figures.caption: Figure 1: Conceptual comparison between conventional image-engineering augmentation and generative augmentation. Conventional operations such as mirroring, rotation, noise, and clipping increase sample count but do not create semantic changes in weather or time. Generative augmentation can change appearance conditions such as fog, rain, snow, and night while preserving the original scene structure.

Figure 5: Comparison of Canny and HED-style edge extraction. Canny preserves fine sign contours and thin structures more explicitly in this example, which is useful when the goal is structural preservation for driving scenes.
Table 8: Comparison with prior work. Positive ∆Ours vs DG means the best proposed model is better than DGInStyle under the metric direction.
Figure 19: Qualitative comparison around PAM. The models change the original appearance toward a snowy road condition, but distant structural consistency differs.
Figure 20: Zoomed example of distant structure preservation. Tune60K weakens road continuity and distorts distant trees, while PAM60K preserves the distant structure more consistently.

Figure 8: Model detail. The feature extractor is initialized from Uni-ControlNet local-control weights and processes three structural conditions. Stable Diffusion, the VAE, and the text encoder are frozen, so training mainly adapts the local control branch.

Table 6: Effect of Uni-ControlNet initialization at 30K steps. Relative change is computed with the pretrained model as the reference, matching the thesis convention. For upward metrics, negative change means the no-pretraining model is worse; for downward metrics, positive change means the no-pretraining model is worse.

Figure 14: Qualitative comparison of training with and without Uni-ControlNet initialization. Without pretraining, road structure and vehicle geometry can collapse or hallucinate. With pretraining, the generated image better preserves road shape and object placement.

Figure 2: Example of the limitation of semantic-mask-only generation, motivated by DGInStyle-style augmentation [39]. If the semantic mask is the only structural condition, details not explicitly constrained by the mask can become weak, and artifacts can appear around road boundaries, vehicles, and other fine structures.

Figure 15: Scaling behavior of structure-related metrics for Tune models. Depth RMSE and edge L1 are lower-is-better; semantic mIoU and object F1 are higher-is-better. Most improvements are largest between 0 and 30K, become milder from 30K to 60K, and approach saturation near 90K.

Figure 4: Comparison of monocular depth maps. ZoeDepth can saturate in distant regions, whereas Metric3Dv2 preserves smoother distant gradients and clearer road-depth structure in this example. Since this work evaluates whether the generated image preserves the original perspective and relative geometry, stable relative depth is important.
Table 4: Main training and inference hyperparameters.
Table 5: Model variants.

Figure 16: Scaling behavior of quality, diversity, and text-alignment metrics for Tune models. CLIP-CMMD is lower-is-better, while R-Precision@5 and LPIPS are higher-is-better. Realism improves until 60K and slightly reverses at 90K.

Figure 17: Qualitative comparison across training steps. Prior-work baselines can show unrealistic style or structural changes, while the Tune models better preserve the original road structure and realism as training proceeds.

Figure 6: Overall multi-condition generation pipeline. The input RGB image is converted into weather/time estimates, semantic segmentation, depth, and edges. A prompt is generated to change appearance, and the prompt plus local conditions are passed to the multi-condition generator.

Figure 7: Prompt-generation pipeline. CLIP estimates the source weather and time subgroup. Semantic segmentation supplies object names. Qwen3-VL generates a caption without explicit weather/time words. A style dictionary then adds target weather/time adjectives and decorations to build the final prompt.

Figure 3: Condition conflict in multi-condition generation. Depth provides low-frequency spatial and distance constraints, while RGB or edge constraints emphasize higher-frequency texture and contour information. When these conditions are injected together without conflict handling, model guidance can become inconsistent and produce distorted or semantically mismatched results.

Figure 10: Common-projection structure evaluation. The original image and generated image are passed through the same structural projectors. Original-side outputs are treated as pseudo ground truth, and generated-side outputs are compared in semantic, depth, edge, and object spaces rather than in raw RGB pixel space.
Table 2: Training datasets.
Table 10: Main PAM settings.
Table 7: Effect of PAM at 60K steps. Relative change is computed with PAM as the reference in the same thesis convention.

Figure 18: Zoomed local artifact that can remain even when training steps are increased. A vehicle in the original can disappear or change into another structure, motivating local condition-conflict suppression.
Table 9: Main components of the base generator without PAM.

Figure 12: Intuition for diversity evaluation. Similar generated image pairs have small distances, while pairs with substantially different weather, road condition, and illumination have larger distances. LPIPS and 1−MS-SSIM quantify this separation.

Figure 13: Intuition for text-alignment evaluation. The correct prompt and 99 mismatched prompts are ranked by CLIP similarity to the generated image. R-Precision is high when the correct prompt appears near the top.

Figure 11: Intuition for realism evaluation. Low-realism images may preserve layout but show unnatural texture, color, or lighting. Higher-realism images show more plausible light, shadow, and atmosphere. CLIPCMMD is smaller for the latter.

Table 1: Scale comparison between large low-level autonomous-driving benchmarks and high-level benchmarks. The numbers are reported as dataset-level descriptions and are used here to motivate annotation-preserving augmentation.

Figure 9: Patch-wise Adaptation Module. Edge, depth, and semantic segmentation are processed by condition-specific stems. At each local feature-grid position, a tri-attention-style gate scores the three conditions, and a straight-through hard selector chooses the locally effective condition before multi-stage control injection.
